nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater streams and igarapés of the Rio Negro and Rio Branco drainages; also recorded from the middle Amazon. Shallow, heavily tannin-stained water with leaf litter, submerged wood, and sparse vegetation over fine sand.

decorPreferences: small ceramic or coconut-shell caves for spawning
clumps of Java moss or other low-light plants to break sight lines

diet.notes: Insectivore in the wild; feeds on small invertebrates, microcrustaceans, and insect larvae. In captivity, live and frozen foods are strongly preferred — bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, baby brine shrimp, and micro worms are all accepted. Prepared flake and micro-pellets may be taken once settled but should not form the core of the diet. Live food is essential for conditioning breeding pairs.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetras, ember tetras)
corydoras adapted to acidic water (C. adolfoi, C. duplicareus)
avoidWith: other Apistogramma species (interspecific aggression and hybridisation risk)
boisterous or fast-moving fish that stress the pair
fin-nipping tetras (blue tetras, Buenos Aires tetras)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo for Apistogramma gephyra; confirm availability and rights before use.

summary: A rare, slender member of the agassizii group from the Rio Negro blackwaters of Brazil. Males sport a distinctive red-edged dorsal fin and rounded caudal. Demands strict blackwater chemistry (pH 4.5–6.0, near-zero hardness) and live foods; recommended for experienced Apistogramma keepers only.
careNotes:
Apistogramma gephyra was described by Kullander (1980) from igarapés of the Archipelago das Anavilhanas in the lower Rio Negro, Amazonas State, Brazil. It belongs to the agassizii species group and is distinguished from A. agassizii by its notably rounder caudal fin and the red seam along the dorsal fin edge (hence the common name 'Redseam Dwarf Cichlid'). Multiple wild geographic forms exist, varying in colour intensity, and most specimens in the hobby are wild-caught or F1 progeny — captive-bred lines remain scarce. The species is rarely seen in the trade and is considered a specialist fish.

Water quality is the single most critical care factor. Wild collection sites consistently measure pH below 4.5 and hardness near 0 dH; in captivity, maintaining pH 4.5–6.0 and gH 0–5 with RO or rain water buffered through peat or botanicals is strongly recommended. Conditioned blackwater can be achieved with Indian almond leaves, alder cones, or peat filtration. Sudden parameter swings must be avoided. Temperature 25–28°C suits most captive situations.

A minimum 80-litre (roughly 20-US-gal) tank is recommended for a haremic group (one male, two or three females). Multiple caves must be provided — one per female — so that spawning territories do not overlap and subordinate females have refuge. Sand substrate allows natural foraging behaviour. The tank should be dimly lit with floating plants or a tannin-stained water column to reduce stress. A quality sponge filter providing gentle flow is preferred over power filters.

This species is strictly carnivorous with a strong preference for moving prey. Live or frozen bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, and baby brine shrimp should form the backbone of the diet. Flake foods are often refused, particularly by newly imported wild fish. Conditioning on live foods is essential before attempting to breed. The species is susceptible to stress-induced disease (hole-in-the-head, bacterial infections) in suboptimal conditions; copper-based medications should be used with extreme caution in soft acidic water.
breedingNotes:
A. gephyra is a haremic cave-spawner. One male maintains a territory encompassing the smaller territories of two to three females, each of which selects her own cave. The female deposits eggs (up to approximately 100–200 per clutch) on the cave ceiling and takes sole responsibility for brooding; she displays a vivid yellow-and-black warning colouration while guarding. The male patrols the outer perimeter and may contribute to fry defence once the brood is free-swimming.

Breeding is most reliably triggered by: strict blackwater chemistry (pH 5.0–5.5 or lower), live-food conditioning of both sexes, gentle filtration, and slightly reduced water-change frequency to stabilise parameters. Fry become free-swimming around day four to five and accept newly hatched brine shrimp, micro worms, and infusoria. Growth is slow; juveniles should be raised in soft, acidic water identical to the breeding tank. Separating multiple broods from different females may be necessary to prevent the dominant female from harassing subordinate broods.

primary.name: FishBase — Apistogramma gephyra
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and biological reference. Provided: family (Cichlidae), order (Cichliformes), native range (lower Rio Negro basin, Amazon basin, Brazil), type locality (Igarapé affluent of Rio Negro, Archipelago das Anavilhanas, 3°00′S 60°45′W), water parameters (pH 5.5–6.0, dH up to 5, temperature 23–30°C), adult size (males 3.3 cm SL, females 4.0 cm), reproduction (cave ceiling deposition, up to 200 eggs, female broods), IUCN Least Concern 2018.

additional.name: DwarfCichlid.com — Apistogramma gephyra
additional.notes: Specialist hobbyist reference. Confirmed agassizii-group membership, multiple wild forms, rarity in trade, wild pH below 4.5, soft blackwater and clear-water habitats in Rio Negro and Rio Branco drainages; diet (live/frozen eagerly taken, flake accepted), haremic keeping, cave-spawning female brood care with occasional male participation, fry diet (BBS, micro worms).

additional.name: Potomac Valley Aquarium Society — Apistogramma gephyra
additional.notes: Hobbyist firsthand account. Confirmed strict live-food preference (tubifex, brine shrimp, daphnia, BBS; flake ignored), pH 4–5 and hardness 1 dH in wild habitat, peat filtration to reach pH ~5 and <2 dH in captivity, 10-gal breeding success, rapid onset of sexual dimorphism, fry free-swimming day 4, female strong parental care.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Dwarf Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma trade care guide; A. gephyra not specifically mentioned. Used for general genus husbandry context: tank size (20 gal for community pairs, 10 gal for breeding), temperature 80–82°F (27–28°C), GH 4–8 dGH for genus, diet (bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, sinking pellets), 1 male per 2–3 females, fry foods (infusoria, micro worms, BBS).

additional.name: TFH Magazine — The Apistogramma Aquarium
additional.notes: Practical Fishkeeping–adjacent specialist article in TFH. Used for blackwater setup methodology (dissolved solids <30 ppm, pH sub-7, sponge filtration, leaf litter, Java moss), live-food conditioning importance, compatible tankmates (rasboras, pencilfish, small tetras, Otocinclus), and species to avoid (blue tetras, Buenos Aires tetras).

fish.breedingNotes: Haremic cave-spawner; female sole brooder, deposits eggs on cave ceiling, clutch up to ~100–200 eggs. Requires strict blackwater chemistry (pH 5.0–5.5 or lower), live-food conditioning, gentle flow, and multiple caves. Fry free-swimming ~day 4–5; accept BBS and micro worms. Captive-bred lines are very scarce — most available fish are wild-caught.
